concept.title: Output Customization Without Plugins
concept.shortdesc: Customize HTML5 and PDF output using built-in DITA-OT parameters, properties files, CSS injection, and DITAVAL filtering — no plugin development required.

section.title: Customization without code
section.p: Many teams assume output customization requires writing XSLT plugins. In practice, DITA-OT provides dozens of built-in parameters that control layout, styling, navigation, and content inclusion — all configurable from the command line, properties files, or project files.
This topic covers every customization you can make without writing any code.

section.title: HTML5 customization parameters
section.p: The HTML5 transtype supports extensive customization through parameters:

row.entry: Custom CSS file injected into every page
Copy the CSS file to the output directory
HTML file included as a page header
HTML file included as a page footer
Navigation TOC style: none, partial, or full
Generate labels for task steps (Prerequisites, Steps, etc.)

section.title: CSS-only branding
section.p: The fastest way to brand HTML5 output is with a custom CSS file. DITA-OT generates semantic HTML with predictable class names, making CSS targeting straightforward:
Save as brand.css and reference via args.css.

section.title: PDF customization parameters
section.p: The PDF transtype also supports customization without XSLT overrides:

row.entry: Add mini-TOC at the start of each chapter
Preserve the bookmap ordering in output

section.title: Properties files
section.p: Instead of passing parameters on the command line, save them in a .properties file:
section.codeblock: # build-html5.properties args.css = brand.css args.cssroot = css args.copycss = yes args.hdr = includes/header.xml nav-toc = full args.indexshow = yes args.gen.task.lbl = YES
section.p: Properties files are version-controllable, shareable, and keep your build commands clean.

section.title: DITAVAL filtering
section.p: DITAVAL files control which content appears in the output based on profiling attributes (audience, platform, product, etc.):
section.codeblock:
<val> <!-- Include only admin content --> <prop action="include" att="audience" val="admin"/> <prop action="exclude" att="audience" val="end-user"/>
<!-- Flag deprecated content with a warning --> <prop action="flag" att="status" val="deprecated"> <startflag imageref="icons/warning.png"> <alt-text>Deprecated</alt-text> </startflag> </prop>
<!-- Highlight new content --> <revprop action="flag" val="2.0" changebar="solid" color="green"/> </val>
section.p: One map + multiple DITAVAL files = multiple audience-specific deliverables with zero content duplication.

section.title: Combining techniques
section.p: The most effective approach combines all three:

ol.li: Properties file — captures all build parameters in a reusable, version-controlled file.
CSS file — handles visual branding for HTML5 without touching the transform.
DITAVAL file — controls content visibility for audience-specific variants.

section.p: Together, these three files give you branded, filtered output with zero plugin development — suitable for 80% of publishing requirements.
